secondary_sort.label: Secondary sort
secondary_sort.summary: Learn how to sort by one column first and then use a second column to break ties in a clear, predictable order.

sketch1.title: The order of columns in ORDER BY matters

sketch2.title: You can mix ASC and DESC

project.title: Practice secondary sorting with product reports

sc-secondary-sort-definition.title: What does a secondary sort do?
sc-secondary-sort-definition.prompt: In `ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC`, what is the job of `price DESC`?
sc-secondary-sort-definition.hint: It is used after SQL compares `category`.

help.concept: In a multi-column ORDER BY, SQL sorts by the first column first. The second column only decides the order for rows that are tied on the first column.
help.hint_1: Think about rows that have the same `category` value.
help.hint_2: The second sort does not reorder the whole table first; it breaks ties inside the first sort.

options.a: It filters out rows with low prices
options.b: It sorts rows within each category from highest price to lowest
options.c: It changes category names into descending order
options.d: It makes SQL ignore the first sort column

sc-order-by-priority.title: Which sort has first priority?
sc-order-by-priority.prompt: Which column has the strongest effect in `ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC`?
sc-order-by-priority.hint: Look at the column listed first.

help.concept: SQL applies multi-column sorting from left to right. The first listed column sets the primary order, and later columns only break ties.
help.hint_1: Ask which column SQL checks before it ever looks at `price`.
help.hint_2: If two rows have different categories, SQL can decide their order without using the second column.

mc-valid-secondary-sort-statements.title: Choose the true statements
mc-valid-secondary-sort-statements.prompt: Which statements about secondary sorting are correct? Choose all that apply.
mc-valid-secondary-sort-statements.hint: Think about left-to-right sorting and tie-breaking.

help.concept: A secondary sort uses additional ORDER BY columns to break ties from earlier columns. The order of columns matters, and each column can have its own direction.
help.hint_1: Check whether the statement describes tie-breaking correctly and whether SQL reads ORDER BY columns left to right.
help.hint_2: Look for statements saying the second column only matters when the first column is equal, and that ASC/DESC can differ by column.

options.a: The second ORDER BY column can break ties from the first column
options.b: ORDER BY price DESC, category ASC always gives the same result as ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC
options.c: You can use ASC for one sort column and DESC for another
options.d: SQL ignores later ORDER BY columns if the first column has repeated values

mc-secondary-sort-examples.title: Identify secondary sort queries
mc-secondary-sort-examples.prompt: Which queries use a secondary sort? Choose all that apply.
mc-secondary-sort-examples.hint: Look for more than one column in ORDER BY.

help.concept: A secondary sort happens when ORDER BY includes multiple columns, such as one main sort and one tie-breaker.
help.hint_1: Count how many columns appear after ORDER BY in each query.
help.hint_2: If a query has only one ORDER BY column, it is not using a secondary sort.

options.a: SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC;
options.b: SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY price ASC;
options.c: SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY stock DESC, name ASC;
options.d: SELECT name FROM products WHERE category = 'Art';

dr-build-secondary-sort-query.title: Build a two-column sort
dr-build-secondary-sort-query.prompt: Put the SQL parts in order to sort products by category A to Z, then by price highest to lowest.
dr-build-secondary-sort-query.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

tokens.t3: ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC;

dr-order-by-clause-sequence.title: Order the sort priorities

fb-secondary-sort-column.title: Fill the tie-breaker column
fb-secondary-sort-column.prompt: Complete the ORDER BY clause so price is used as the second sort.
fb-secondary-sort-column.hint: The blank should be the second column name.

help.concept: A secondary sort is written by adding another column after the first ORDER BY column, separated by a comma.
help.hint_1: The first sort is already `category ASC`; the blank should name the column used after that.
help.hint_2: Use the numeric column that would sort items from more expensive to less expensive.

fb-secondary-sort-direction.title: Fill the direction
fb-secondary-sort-direction.prompt: Complete the clause so names are sorted from A to Z after stock is sorted highest first.
fb-secondary-sort-direction.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

secondary-sort-basics.title: Why use a secondary sort?
secondary-sort-basics.bodyMarkdown:
A **secondary sort** means you sort by one column first, then by another column when rows are tied on the first one.

In SQL, you do this by listing more than one column in `ORDER BY`.

```sql
SELECT name, category, price
FROM products
ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC;
```

This query sorts the `products` table in two steps:

1. `category ASC` puts rows in alphabetical category order: `Art`, `Home`, `Stationery`.
2. `price DESC` is used **inside each category** so higher-priced items appear first within that category.

With the sample data, the `Art` rows would appear with `Acrylic Paint Set` before `Sketchbook`, because both are in `Art` and `24.50` is greater than `12.99`.

So the main idea is: **the first sort decides the big groups, and the next sort decides the order inside those groups**.

secondary-sort-order-matters.title: The order of columns in ORDER BY matters
secondary-sort-order-matters.bodyMarkdown:
When you use multiple columns in `ORDER BY`, SQL reads them from left to right.

Compare these two queries:

```sql
SELECT name, category, price
FROM products
ORDER BY category ASC, price DESC;
```

```sql
SELECT name, category, price
FROM products
ORDER BY price DESC, category ASC;
```

They do **not** mean the same thing.

In the first query:
- SQL groups the rows by `category` order first.
- Then it sorts by `price` inside each category.

In the second query:
- SQL looks at `price` first across the whole table.
- `category` only breaks ties when two rows have the same price.

Worked example from this dataset:
- `Office Chair` has price `129.00`, so it comes first if you sort by `price DESC` first.
- But if you sort by `category ASC` first, all `Art` rows come before all `Home` rows, even though `Office Chair` is the most expensive item.

secondary-sort-mixed-directions.title: You can mix ASC and DESC
secondary-sort-mixed-directions.bodyMarkdown:
Each column in `ORDER BY` can have its own direction.

```sql
SELECT name, stock, created_at
FROM products
ORDER BY stock DESC, created_at ASC;
```

Line by line:

- `SELECT name, stock, created_at` shows the columns we want to inspect.
- `FROM products` reads from the `products` table.
- `ORDER BY stock DESC, created_at ASC` sorts by highest stock first, and if two rows have the same stock, the older date comes first.

This is useful when you want a ranking with a tie-breaker.

For example, `Notebook` has stock `50`, so it appears before items with lower stock. If two products had the same stock, `created_at ASC` would place the earlier date first.
